chain_of_thought: Native stack trace only shows unknown frames plus the JNI bridge; no allocator/sanitizer details or faulting instruction are provided, so I cannot identify the first application-level native frame below allocators or decompile it.

I attempted to use Ghidra MCP to locate and decompile Java_com_radaee_pdf_Document_openMem and list functions in librdpdf.so, but the MCP backend returned HTTP 404 errors, so no native function bodies are available for inspection.

Without decompiled code, I cannot trace memcpy/memmove usage, pointer arithmetic, or length calculations, nor can I perform the mandatory backward data-flow from a crashing instruction to arguments, or identify whether any buffer sizes are derived from attacker-controlled input.

The Java-side call graph is also not provided beyond the single jni_bridge_method name, and Jadx MCP was not invoked due to the instructions requiring Ghidra first; thus I cannot see how openMem is called, what arguments (byte arrays, lengths, offsets) are passed, or whether they come from untrusted sources vs. well-formed internal data.

The fuzzer entrypoint fuzz_one_input suggests this crash was triggered by arbitrary fuzz data reaching the JNI method, which in principle could expose memory-safety bugs (e.g., PDF parsing in librdpdf.so is often vulnerable), but there is no concrete evidence in this specific crash: no signal (SIGSEGV, SIGABRT), no allocator diagnostics, no indication of OOB access or UAF, and even the number of native frames is unknown (marked as ??).

Given the lack of any concrete crash mechanism, faulting address, or code snippet, I cannot responsibly assert the presence of a particular CWE or exploitable condition; at best I can say that this area of code is historically risky and merits further review, but this particular CrashEntry is too incomplete for classification.

Therefore, I must classify this as not confirmed vulnerable, with low confidence either way, and recommend re-running the fuzzer with full symbolized backtraces and enabling sanitizers (ASan/UBSan) to capture a future crash with sufficient detail for analysis.
reasons: Crash record lacks signal/abort reason, faulting PC, and concrete native frames (all marked ??).
Ghidra MCP access to librdpdf.so functions failed (HTTP 404), so no native implementation or data-flow can be inspected.
Without code or stack details, there is no specific evidence of memory corruption or logic vulnerability tied to attacker-controlled input.
recommendations: Re-run fuzzing with full symbolized backtraces and record precise crash signal, PC, and registers.
Enable ASan/UBSan or equivalent on librdpdf.so to capture detailed diagnostics for any memory errors in Java_com_radaee_pdf_Document_openMem.
Once a concrete crash with full native stack and faulting code is captured, repeat triage with working Ghidra/Jadx MCP to inspect data-flow from JNI inputs to unsafe operations.
assumptions: Fuzzer passes arbitrary data into Java_com_radaee_pdf_Document_openMem but actual argument types and validation are unknown.
The missing stack frames (??) could include critical allocator or sanitizer functions not visible here.
MCP backend access problems are environmental, not indicative of missing symbols in the binary itself.
limitations: No faulting instruction, register state, or signal type was provided.
Could not decompile any native functions in librdpdf.so due to MCP HTTP 404 errors.
No Java call graph or Jadx context beyond the JNI method name was available, preventing taint analysis from Java to native.
